coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Abhishek
Verma, Navneet Chandra
Khan, Syamantak
Nandi, Chayan Kanti
coredata.dc:description: A novel one-step method for the synthesis of bright, multicolor fluorescent sulphur doped carbon dots (CNDs) has been developed by using simple microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and sodium thiosulphate. The synthesized CNDs showed dual mode naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive sensing capability both for arsenic [As (III)] and glutathione (GSH) with high selectivity. Using fluorometric assay, the detection limit (DL) for As (III) was found to be as low as 32pM. The selectivity data show that the newly developed CNDs is very specific for As (III) even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. The CNDs were also able to detect GSH very selectively over other biothiols like cysteine (Cys) and homo-cysteine (H-cys) with a DL of 43nM, even in blood plasma. The fast kinetic data suggests that the present CNDs assay could be used onsite As (III) detection. The CNDs, further, showed its potential application in high resolution bioimaging of bacterial nucleoid segregation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colorimetric dual mode sensor
Arsenic and glutathione detection
Bacterial Nucleoid division

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
Detection method for As (III) in water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of the synthesized CNDs, inset showing the height profile and average size of the CNDs (b) TEM image of synthesized CNDs, inset showing particle size distribution and structure of a single CND (c) Raman shift spectrum, (inset: the XRD pattern of synthesized CNDs) and (d) FTIR spectra of synthesized CNDs showing the major functional groups such as –OH, –SH, C=O, –COOH and C–S–C group.

(a) XPS spectrum of the as synthesized CNDs shows three major peaks of C1s, O1s and S2p respectively. (b), (c) and (d) Showed the high resolution C1s, O1s and S2p spectra of the as synthesized CNDs for characterizing the different functional groups (–OH, C=O, –COOH, C–S–C and
SO X
2 −
).
Schematics representation of the yellow precipitation, which contains the products P1 [(CND-GS)3As(III)OH3], P2 [(CND-S)3 As(III)OH3], P3 [H3As(V)OS3] and P4 [As2S3].

(a) A Jablonski diagram for the formation of non-fluorescent dark state in presence of MV (electron acceptor) which is hindered by AA (electron donor) and (b) STORM image in E. coli bacterial cell. The left panel (white) shows the image taken directly from EMCCD and the right panel shows its reconstructed image. The colour bar shows the intensity of the localized points.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology, Mandi, Himachal Pradesh 175001, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel one-step method for the synthesis of bright, multicolor fluorescent sulphur doped carbon dots (CNDs) has been developed by using simple microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and sodium thiosulphate. The synthesized CNDs showed dual mode naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive sensing capability both for arsenic [As (III)] and glutathione (GSH) with high selectivity. Using fluorometric assay, the detection limit (DL) for As (III) was found to be as low as 32pM. The selectivity data show that the newly developed CNDs is very specific for As (III) even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. The CNDs were also able to detect GSH very selectively over other biothiols like cysteine (Cys) and homo-cysteine (H-cys) with a DL of 43nM, even in blood plasma. The fast kinetic data suggests that the present CNDs assay could be used onsite As (III) detection. The CNDs, further, showed its potential application in high resolution bioimaging of bacterial nucleoid segregation.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), on the other hand, is the most abundant cellular biothiols and essential endogenous antioxidants. It plays a central role in cellular defence against toxins and free radicals (Franco and Cidlowski, 2009). Abnormal levels of GSH can lead to cancer, aging, heart problems and other ailments (Michelet et al., 1995). Unfortunately, because of the structural similarity with the other biothiol such as Cys and H-cys, it is still a major challenge to detect GSH selectively with high sensitivity and specificity (Niu et al., 2012; Yin et al., 2014; Ju et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2014).

To address the above problems, we have demonstrated a simple but elegant approach by synthesizing sulphur doped CNDs for the dual mode naked eye colorimetric As (III) and GSH detection in aqueous real water system and even in complex plasma matrix. The CNDs are new class of carbogenic nanomaterials that have attracted tremendous interest in recent years, because of the several advantages such as high solubility, resistance to photo bleaching, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility (Luo et al., 2013; Zerda et al., 2008). Furthermore, unlike the semiconductor quantum dots or metal nanoparticles, CNDs could easily be synthesized using a single step reaction. Chemical doping in nanomaterials can tailor its electronic property and chemical reactivity (Ding et al., 2014; Choi et al., 2012). For example, nitrogen doped graphene exhibited high CO tolerance for oxygen reduction reaction (Yang et al., 2012). Recently, nitrogen doped CNDs have shown their potential application on ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ (Gupta et al., 2015; Zhang and Chen, 2014). However, the origin of multicolor fluorescence of CNDs is not clear till date and remains a topic of debate. The recent studies show that the presence of different surface defects and energy traps are the most probable origin of fluorescence (Wang et al., 2010). Whether the fluorescence originates from a single particle with multichromophoric groups or from different particles, is still debatable (Das et al., 2014).

A novel single step method have been established to synthesize bright, fluorescent CNDs using microwave assisted pyrolysis. Here, 25mL trisodium citrate solution (0.1M) and 25mL of sodium thiosulphate (0.1M) were added into a 50mL beaker. The mixture was heated inside a domestic microwave at 600W powers for 6min. The resulting material was dissolved in 50mL of distilled water. The solution was filtered by Whatman filter paper. 1.5mL of the filtered solution was centrifuged twice (Sorvall Lynx 6000, thermo scientific) at 23,000rpm for 30min each. The resulting pellets were removed every time and the final supernatant was collected and used for experiments. Atomic Force Microscopy, Transmission and Scanning Electron Microscopy (AFM, TEM, SEM), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and various other spectroscopic analysis such as UV–vis, High Resolution Mass Spectroscopy (HRMS), Fourier Transformed Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman Spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to characterize the synthesized material. The prepared fluorescent CNDs are water soluble and showed strong multicolor fluorescence. The DL for As (III) and GSH using the CNDs was found to be as low as 32pM and 43nM, respectively. In addition GSH detection was very selective over the other biothiols. Further, the CNDs showed its potential application in high resolution bioimaging of bacterial nucleoid segregation.

All glasswares were washed with aqua regia (3 HCl: 1 HNO3), followed by rinsing several times with double distilled water. Sodium citrate, Sodium thiosulfate, GSH, methyl viologen (MV), ascorbic acid (AA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), blood plasma, Cysteine (Cys), Homo-Cysteine (H-cys), 3-mercapto propanoic acid (MPA), tartaric acid (TA), 3-mercapto succinic acid (MSA), S-hexyl glutathione and ethyl ester reduced glutathione were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. All metal ions including chloride of lead, mercury, chromium, cadmium, magnesium, copper, cobalt, nickel, manganese, zinc and arsenic oxide with purity >99% were purchased from Merck India chemicals. Double distilled 18.3mΩ deionized (DI) water (Elga Purelab Ultra, Vivendi Water Systems Ltd.) was used throughout for the preparation of solutions.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Shimadzu UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu Corporation. Japan. The spectra were collected using a quartz cuvette of 10mm path length and volume 1mL. All the measurements were repeated at least three times.

FTIR spectra of CNDs and GSH/CNDs were measured using a Agilent FTIR spectrophotometer, (Model No. K8002AA carry 660, USA) equipped with a horizontal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory containing a zinc selenide crystal and operating at 4cm− 1 resolution. Sample preparation done by putting the dry CNDs powder on ATR plate followed by scanning from 400 to 4000cm− 1.

AFM analysis of the synthesized CNDs for particle size determination was carried out using a Bruker AFM Instruments (Model – Dimension Icon). Standard Veeco tapping mode silicon probes were used for scanning the samples. Typically, aqueous suspensions of CNDs samples were dried on silicon substrate for 3h. Once dried, samples were placed on the AFM stage and scanned.

X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) with auger electron spectroscopy (AES) (Model No. PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, Chanhassen, MN) FEI Inc. and C60 sputter gun have been used for characterization and scanning the spectra from C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p region. Al Kα X-ray radiation was used as the source for excitation (1486.8eV, 500mm). Samples were loaded on copper strips, and surface adherence done by double sided adhesive tape.

The particle size and dispersity of the synthesized CNDs were performed using a TECNAI G2 200kV TEM (FEI, Electron Optics) electron microscope with 200kV input voltage. TEM grids were prepared by placing 5µL diluted and well sonicated sample solution on a carbon coated copper grid and evaporated the solution at room temperature completely.

XRD diffraction patterns were recorded on Rigaku Smart Lab X-Ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation as X-ray source (λ=1.5418Å) at room temperature. The voltage and current for the measurement were kept 45kV and 100mA respectively. 1mL of dialysed CNDs were dried inside a 37°C incubator to get powder sample for analysis.

The Raman spectrum was measured by the confocal Microscope Raman spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, XploRA ONE) with 633nm laser. Sample preparation for analysis was done by putting the drop of CNDs on the quartz slide and followed by drying inside a 37°C incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para:
Steady state fluorescence was measured using Horiba Fluorolog-3 Spectrofluorometer. All the experiments including
AsO 2 −
detection were performed at room temperature after equilibrating the samples for 15min. The fluorescence spectra were measured in 1mL quartz cuvette. For titration experiment, to calculate the detection limit, different concentration of
AsO 2 −
ions were added to a fixed concentration of CNDs and the final volume is maintained as 1mL. For determining the selectivity for [
AsO 2 −
] ion, 50μM of each metal ion were added into a fixed concentration of GSH/CNDs. The kinetics of the detection was investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of GSH/CNDs, immediately after the addition of [
AsO 2 −
] ion upto 5min (as most of the quenching processes were over within 5min of the contact time). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Horiba scientific Delta Flex TCSPC system (Serial No. 11385, Scotland) with Pulsed LED Sources. Ludox has been used to measure an instrument response function (IRF) for de-convolution of the spectral value. The photon decays in different channels were fitted tri-exponentially with a chi-squared value <1.2 in order to calculate fluorescence lifetime. Approximately 3mL CNDs solution has been used for all measurements.

The average size of the CNDs was found to be ~4 to 5nm as confirmed by the height profile in the AFM image and the size distribution of more than seventy spots in TEM image ( Fig. 1a and b, insets). The SEM image and the DLS results (Fig. S1) further supports the size of the CNDs. The two closely packed peaks at 1580cm−1 (G band) and 1335cm−1 (D band) in Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c), representing the E2g mode of graphite and the disordered structure of graphene, respectively (Wang et al., 2014). The amorphous nature of the CNDs are confirmed by XRD pattern (inset, Fig. 1c), which showed a broad diffraction peak at 24.7 θ (Sun et al., 2013). The HRMS of the CNDs showed a native peak at m/z=576 (Fig. S2a). The peaks at 1650, 1594 and 1390cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d) suggest the presence of C=O, C–O and –COOH stretching. Peak at 658cm−1 for C–S–/C–S–C bending and the broad peak at 3400cm−1 is attributed to –OH stretching vibrations.

The XPS ( Fig. 2) data confirm the presence of –OH, –COOH, C=O, C–S–C and
–
SO X
2 −
surface functional groups in CNDs. The three peaks at 162.0, 283.7 and 530.1eV in XPS spectrum can be attributed to S2p, C1s and O1s, respectively suggesting that the synthesized CNDs contained C, O and S with % atom ratio of C: O: S=33.1: 54.5: 12.4. The three peaks in the high resolution XPS of C1s spectrum at 283.2, 284.6 and 287.9eV are assigned to C–C, C–O and C–O/C=O, respectively. The two peaks at 529.8 and 533.8eV in O1s spectrum are assigned to –OH and C–OH/COOH groups, while the four peaks at 160.5, 161.8, 166.3 and 167.7eV in S2p spectrum are assigned to the C–S–C and
–
SO X
2 −
groups, respectively. The doping concentration of sulphur (S2p) is 12.4% in the synthesized CNDs as calculated by XPS elemental analysis.

A usual multicolor fluorescence was observed with the emission maximum at 425nm, when excited at 300nm. Very low fluorescence, when excited at the absorption maximum (220nm) suggests that the absorbed species are mainly non-emissive in nature (Fig. S3) (Galande et al., 2011). Further, the measured excitation spectra showed two distinct set of bands upon changing the emission positions, possibly suggesting the presence of two emissive species. Recently, we and others have reported such observation (Strauss et al., 2014; Nie et al., 2014). Although the origin of such multicolor fluorescence is still debatable because of its unknown structure, the inhomogeneous broadening and spectral migration suggested as one of the key origin (Khan et al., 2015a,b). The commonly observed red-shifting phenomena in CNDs originates from an individual entity with an ensemble of energy sub-states, when solvent relaxation and fluorescence lifetime matches with each other (Fig. S4). The chemical environment, such as pH, temperature and concentration also played important role on the fluorescence of the CNDs. Reversible photoswitching in CNDs with an electron transfer process has also been proposed, recently by our group (Khan et al., 2015a,b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection method for As (III) in water
ce:section.ce:para: For ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of As (III), the synthesized CNDs were functionalized with GSH (Delnomdedieu et al., 1993). The functionalization of GSH on CNDs surface was confirmed by the appearance of a new peak in HRMS at m/z=881, which is m/z=305 (losing two protons from parent GSH with m/z=307) higher than the parent CNDs peak at m/z=576 (Fig. S2b). The attachment of GSH to CNDs was further confirmed by the appearance of a new peak in the FTIR spectrum at 2527cm−1 for the –SH group (Fig. S5a) and little increase in size in the AFM image (~5nm) (Fig. S6). When GSH modified CNDs (GSH/CNDs) were equilibrated with As (III) (50µM) for 3min, an immediate yellow precipitation (inset Fig. S7a) along with extensive quenching of fluorescence was observed ( Fig. 3a). The inset in Fig. 3a showed the UV lamp image of the GSH/CNDs and in addition of As (III). This indicated the interaction and the complex formation in between GSH/CNDs and As (III), which may be attributed to the excited state electron transfer process, confirmed by the change in the excited state lifetime (Fig. S8).

The sensitivity of the GSH/CNDs to As (III) was tested by titrimetric experiment followed by calculating the detection limit (DL). The DL was found as 32pM at a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 and a fitting parameter of 0.998 (Fig. 3b and inset). Only few reports are available on such ultrasensitive naked eye as well as colorimetric detection, but none using CNDs (Wu et al., 2012; Kalluri et al., 2009; Sirawatcharin et al., 2014). The fast kinetic data confirm the potential application of the CNDs for on-site As (III) detection (Fig. 3c). The practical applicability of the system was tested by calculating the DL of As (III) in real water samples, where several contaminations are present (the physicochemical composition of the river and tap water are given in Tables S2 and S3). The DL for As (III) in the river and tap water was found to be 48pM and 46pM, respectively (Fig. S9a and b).

Apart from Fe3+, the GSH/CNDs sensor was highly selective for As (III) over several other metal ions (Fig.s 3d and S10). The DL of Fe3+ was found to be 59nM (Fig. S11a). Interestingly, Fe3+ detection was also possible with bare CNDs (without GSH addition) with a DL of 73nM (Fig. S11b). Further, no such colour change was observed in this case suggesting a different mechanism, which does not interfere with As (III) detection (Qu et al., 2013; Ju and Chen, 2014).

In an alternative way the present CNDs were also able to detect GSH over Cys and H-cys very efficiently. To this end, the CNDs initially were incubated with 50µM of As (III) for 15min following by the addition of GSH. The titrimetric experiment showed the extensive fluorescence quenching with the DL of 43nM ( Fig. 4a). Recently, a dual mode nanosensor assay using carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for discriminative GSH detection with a DL of 50nM has been developed (Shi et al., 2014). For more biological relevance, we performed the GSH detection in CNDs/As (III) solution in presence of human blood plasma solution. A clear yellow precipitation as well extensive fluorescence quenching with a DL of 88nM, confirms the ultrasensitive detection of GSH (Figs. S4b and S12).

The specificity for GSH was further tested with various others amino acids (which do not contain any thiol group) (Fig. 4c) but no interference was observed. The kinetics of GSH detection was also very fast (within 10min) (Fig. 4d). Interestingly, this phenomenon was not observed with either Cys or H-cys, which belong the same class of biothiols family (Fig. S13).

The observed yellow precipitation could be mixture of several products (P1, P2, P3 and P4) followed by the possible reactions (R1 to R6) as shown in Fig. 5 (Hug et al., 2014). In the first step both the protons of the two –COOH groups of GSH, (because of the very low pKa=2.12 and 3.59), will be released and a simultaneous attachment of GSH to CNDs will occur (confirmed from mass spectroscopic and FTIR data) (R1). These released protons will help in zwitterion formation with the –NH2 group of GSH, releasing H2S/elemental Sulphur (R2) and the reduction of
SO X
2 −
groups in CNDs (CNDs
SO X
2 −
) to S-(CNDs-S-) (Couture et al., 2013; Muyzer and Stams, 2008) (R3) (Fig. S7a and b). The R2 and R3 pathway was only possible upon addition of As2O3 where it converted to H3As(III)O3 (R4) (Scott et al., 1993). R2 may be preceded by the conversion of CNDs–S to CNDs–O when a C–S–C is converted into C–O–C bonds as confirmed by the broad peak at 805cm−1 in FTIR for oxirane (Fig. S5b).

The presence of CNDs in the yellow precipitation (P1 and P2) was confirmed by the similarity in the FTIR data with the bare CNDs (Fig. S5c). Product P3 and P4 will also form following the reaction R5 and R6 (Hug et al., 2014). It is noteworthy that the reaction R5 and R6 was also exclusively dependent on As2O3 addition (Fig. S7c and d). Several control experiments shows the crucial role of GSH for As (III) detection, especially the two –COOH groups, one thiol group and the skeletal structure of GSH. For example, when Cys, H-cys, 3-mercapto propionic acid (with only one –COOH and one –SH group) and tartaric acid (one –NH2 and two –COOH, but no –SH group) were used instead of GSH, no significant changes in the colour or fluorescence spectra were observed (Fig. S13). When 3-mercapto succinic acid (two –COOH and one thiol group but a smaller entity than GSH) was used, very small changes were observed (Fig. S13). Finally, S-Hexyl Glutathione and Glutathione reduced ethyl ester (H in –SH was replaced by alkyl chain and –COOH was replaced by ester) showed not observable changes (Fig. S14).

In conclusion, sulphur doped CNDs were synthesized in a facile single step method for the ultrasensitive colorimetric naked eye detection of As (III) with a DL down to 32pM. The selectivity experiments shows that the colorimetric and fluorescent sensor is highly specific for As (III) even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. In an alternative way the CNDs were also able to detect GSH (DL=43nM) very selectively over Cys and H-cys. The CNDs could not only function in aqueous solution but also exhibit sensitive responses toward GSH in complicated biological environments (blood plasma). The fast kinetic data showed that the current assay could efficiently be used for the on-site real time detection of As (III). The CNDs also showed single molecule photoswitching and potential application in localization based blinking microscopy for high resolution bio-imaging. Further research is much needed to understand the real structure, the origin of multicolor photoluminescence centre of carbon dot. The photoswitching mechanism and the binding affinity towards specific cellular component requires an extensive research for its real application in super resolution microscopy.
All authors have declared that no competing interest exists.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the home institute (IIT Mandi) and Department of Biotechnology (BT/PR4067/BRB/10/1128/2012) for running the experiments. The support of Advanced Materials Research Center (AMRC), IIT Mandi is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2016.03.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
